section.title: Paul's Appeal for Onesimus
section.sectionRationale: This is the heart of the letter. Paul, who could command, chooses to appeal on the basis of love. He introduces Onesimus as his own child in the faith, plays on the meaning of his name, and asks Philemon to receive back the runaway no longer as a slave but as a beloved brother.
section.thesis: Paul refuses to coerce and instead pleads in love for Onesimus, a once useless runaway now made useful in Christ, so that the gospel might turn a master and his slave into brothers, quietly dissolving the old order from the inside.
section.frameNote: One movement in four beats: appeal by love not command; the introduction of Onesimus as Paul's child and heart; the reason for sending him back; and the transformation from slave to beloved brother.

verses.text: So although in Christ I am bold enough to order you to do what is proper,
I prefer to {{parakaleo|appeal}} on the basis of love. For I, Paul, am now aged, and a prisoner of Christ Jesus as well.
I appeal to you for my {{teknon|child}} {{onesimos|Onesimus}}, whose father I became while I was in chains.
Formerly he was {{achrestos|useless}} to you, but now he has become {{euchrestos|useful}} both to you and to me.
I am sending back to you him who is my very {{splagchna|heart}}.

units.label: Sent back with consent, not compulsion

verses.text: I would have liked to keep him with me, so that on your behalf he could minister to me in my chains for the gospel.
But I did not want to do anything without your consent, so that your goodness will not be out of compulsion, but by your own free will.

units.label: No longer a slave, but a brother

verses.text: For perhaps this is why he was separated from you for a while, so that you might have him back for good:
no longer as a {{doulos|slave}}, but better than a slave, as a beloved brother. He is especially beloved to me, but even more so to you, both in person and in the Lord.

parakaleo.gloss: To appeal, urge, exhort, comfort, or come alongside. Though in Christ he is bold enough to command, Paul deliberately chooses to appeal, to plead by love rather than compel by authority. The word signals the whole method of the letter: persuasion born of love in place of coercion.

teknon.gloss: A child. Paul calls Onesimus his child, whose father he became while in chains, meaning he led Onesimus to faith in Christ during his imprisonment. The word conveys tender spiritual fatherhood and stakes Paul's own heart in the request.

onesimos.gloss: Onesimus, a common name given to slaves, meaning useful or beneficial. Paul plays on its meaning: the man named Useful had once been useless, but in Christ has become truly useful, living up to his name at last. The pun carries the good news that Christ makes people what they were meant to be.

achrestos.gloss: Useless, unprofitable, good for nothing. Paul uses it of Onesimus before his conversion, a pointed play on the name Useful. The root chrestos sounds much like Christos, Christ, so many hear a further echo: apart from Christ he was useless, but in Christ he is not.

euchrestos.gloss: Useful, serviceable, profitable, of good use. Now, transformed by the gospel, Onesimus has become useful both to Philemon and to Paul. The word completes the wordplay and pictures the practical fruit of a changed life in Christ.

splagchna.gloss: Literally the inward parts, used figuratively for the deepest affections, the heart. Paul says Onesimus is his very splagchna; to send him back is to send his own heart. The word binds Paul's love for Onesimus to the refreshing of hearts he praised in verse 7 and will ask for again in verse 20.

doulos.gloss: A slave or bondservant, someone owned and bound to serve. It names the social reality Paul is quietly overturning: Onesimus is to be received no longer merely as a slave but as a beloved brother, a new relationship in which the old category can no longer hold the same meaning.

basic.context: Now Paul comes to the real reason for the letter. He could simply order Philemon to do the right thing, but he chooses instead to ask, out of love. He introduces Onesimus as his own child in the faith, converted while Paul was in prison. He plays on Onesimus's name, which means useful: once he was useless to Philemon, now he is genuinely useful. Onesimus is being sent back, and Paul asks Philemon to welcome him not as a runaway slave but as a beloved brother.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul does not directly tell Philemon to free Onesimus, and he does not call slavery good. Instead he plants something more powerful: once master and slave are brothers in Christ, the whole relationship is changed from the inside. The letter shows how the gospel undermines slavery at its root, by making the two men family, even while Paul works within the world as it was.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul says he would rather appeal, parakaleo, than command. He calls Onesimus his teknon, his child. He puns on the name Onesimus, useful: once achrestos, useless, now euchrestos, useful, and the word for useless even echoes the name of Christ. Onesimus is Paul's own splagchna, his heart, and he is to be received no longer as a mere doulos, a slave, but as a brother.
basic.typology: Paul sending back the one who is his very heart, and asking that he be received, pictures the gospel: Christ takes in the useless and the runaway, makes them useful and beloved, and presents them to the Father as His own. Onesimus the runaway welcomed home is a small image of every sinner brought home in Christ.
basic.christ: The name play points to Christ. Apart from Christ, Onesimus was useless; in Christ he is made new and useful. Christ is the one who seeks the runaway, changes the heart, and turns strangers into family. Paul's tender love for his converted child mirrors the Savior's love for the lost He redeems.
basic.god: God works through even a slave's flight and a prison cell to bring a man to Himself, and He remakes enemies and strangers into brothers. God is shown as the One whose grace reaches the lowest and the least, giving them a new name and a new family.
basic.crossReferences: Colossians 3:11 says in Christ there is no slave or free. First Corinthians 7:22 calls the slave the Lord's freedman. Luke 15 tells of a son who was lost and is found, welcomed home, much like Onesimus.
basic.oneStory: The gospel does not merely improve the old order; it makes new people who cannot see one another the old way again. In turning a slave and his master into brothers, this passage shows the reconciling power that runs through the whole Bible and will one day heal every division.

context.literary: The body and climax of the letter. Paul's rhetoric is careful and warm: he first disarms by declining to command, then names Onesimus only after building affection, saving the name for verse 10. The wordplay in verse 11 is the hinge, and the movement rises to its point in verse 16, no longer a slave but a beloved brother.
context.historical: Onesimus was Philemon's slave who had run away, quite possibly having wronged or robbed him, a capital-level offense under Roman law where runaways could be branded, imprisoned, or killed. He somehow reached Paul in prison, was converted, and is now being sent back, which was legally required, but with a letter that transforms the terms of his return. Roman slavery was widespread and not race based, an economic and social institution into which people fell through debt, war, or birth.
context.canonical: The letter became a touchstone in later debates over slavery. Read rightly, it does not endorse the institution but sows the seed of its undoing: by making master and slave brothers, equally beloved and equally in the Lord, it plants a truth that slavery cannot finally survive.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To win, not force, Philemon's glad reception of Onesimus as a brother, by appealing to love, by staking his own affection on the man, and by reframing the whole relationship in the light of Christ, so that grace rather than law governs the outcome.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul describes and requests a specific reconciliation; he does not prescribe a rule about legal manumission. What he prescribes, and it is revolutionary, is that believers must regard one another as beloved brothers regardless of social station, a truth that quietly dismantles the logic of slavery.

debates.question: Does Philemon endorse or undermine slavery?

views.label: It undermines slavery from within
views.case: By insisting Onesimus be received no longer as a slave but as a beloved brother, both in person and in the Lord, Paul plants a truth incompatible with owning him. Colossians 3:11 and 1 Corinthians 7 point the same way; the gospel dissolves the relationship from the inside rather than by decree.

views.label: It merely regulates an existing institution
views.case: Paul works within Roman law, sends the slave back, and does not explicitly demand freedom, which some read as tacit acceptance of slavery as a social given rather than a call to abolish it.

debates.bearing: The stronger reading is that Paul plants the seed of abolition: he does not overthrow Rome by force, but he makes a brotherhood that cannot coexist with bondage. The New Testament ethic he sets in motion undid slavery wherever it was truly believed.

debates.question: Is Paul hinting that Philemon should set Onesimus free?

views.label: Yes, by strong implication
views.case: Better than a slave, the hope that Philemon will do even more than asked in verse 21, and the offer to repay all debts, together nudge Philemon toward manumission without commanding it, leaving the costly choice to love.

views.label: The main aim is reconciliation, not manumission
views.case: Paul's explicit request is that Onesimus be welcomed as a brother; freedom may follow, but the letter's heart is restored relationship rather than a change of legal status.

debates.bearing: Whether or not freedom is formally granted, the relationship can never be the same; a brother beloved in the Lord cannot be treated as mere property, and love is left to draw the obvious conclusion.

study.originalLanguages: The words carry the argument. Paul could command, but prefers to parakaleo, to appeal in love. He names Onesimus his teknon, his child, born to him in chains. Then comes the pun: Onesimus means useful, yet he had been achrestos, useless, and is now euchrestos, useful, the wordplay turning on a changed life, with the root chrestos echoing Christos, Christ, so that apart from Christ he was useless and in Christ he is not. Onesimus is Paul's splagchna, his very heart. And the goal is that he be received no longer as a doulos, a slave, but as a beloved brother. Every key term bends toward reconciliation.

typology.type: The runaway received back no longer as a slave but as a beloved brother
typology.fulfillment: The gospel welcome of sinners: Christ takes in the useless and the lost, makes them useful and beloved, and brings them home as family, no longer slaves but sons, Galatians 4:7.

typology.type: Onesimus as Paul's very heart, sent to be received
typology.fulfillment: A picture of the beloved sent and welcomed; as Paul stakes his heart on Onesimus, so the Father gives what is dearest that the estranged might be received.

study.christ: The wordplay preaches Christ: apart from Him a man is useless, in Him he is made new and truly useful. Christ is the one who pursues the runaway, converts him in a prison cell, and turns a fugitive into a beloved brother. Paul's fatherly love for Onesimus and his willingness to send away the one who is his own heart mirror the Savior who loves the lost and gives what is dearest to bring them home. The whole appeal only makes sense because in Christ there is neither slave nor free.
study.god: God is at work in the least likely places, using a slave's flight, a wrong committed, and an apostle's chains to bring one man to salvation. He reaches to the lowest and the seemingly useless, gives them a new heart and a new family, and delights to remake strangers and even enemies into brothers. This is the God whose grace levels every social wall in Christ.

crossReferences.note: In Christ there is no Greek or Jew, slave or free; Christ is all and in all, the truth that reframes Onesimus and Philemon.
The one called as a slave is the Lord's freedman, and the free person is Christ's slave, relativizing all earthly status.
There is neither slave nor free, for all are one in Christ Jesus, the principle undoing slavery from within.
The lost son welcomed home by a running father, an image of Onesimus received back and of every sinner brought home.

study.oneStory: The gospel does not merely reform the old order; it creates new people who can no longer see one another the old way. By turning a slave and his master into beloved brothers, this passage displays the reconciling power that runs through the whole Bible, from a divided humanity toward the day when every wall is down and the redeemed of every station are one family in Christ.

sermon.title: On the Basis of Love
sermon.why: The central message of Begg's series A Study in Philemon takes exactly this passage, Philemon 1:8-16, showing how Paul appeals on the basis of love rather than authority and how the gospel transforms Onesimus from useless runaway to beloved brother. A clear, orthodox exposition of this movement's climax.

quiz.q: Why does Paul choose to appeal to Philemon rather than command him?

options.text: He wants Philemon's goodness to be free and from love, not forced by authority.
options.note: Paul says he prefers to appeal on the basis of love so Philemon acts by his own free will, verses 9 and 14.

options.text: He is not sure he actually has any authority over Philemon.
options.note: Paul says he is bold enough to command; he simply chooses not to, for love's sake.

options.text: He is afraid Philemon will refuse a direct order.
options.note: Paul is confident of Philemon's obedience; his motive is love, not fear.

options.text: He thinks commands are always wrong.
options.note: Paul can and does give commands elsewhere; here he chooses appeal to honor Philemon's willing love.

quiz.q: What is the point of Paul's wordplay on the name Onesimus?

options.text: Onesimus means useful; once he was useless, but in Christ he has become truly useful.
options.note: The pun in verse 11 pictures how the gospel transforms a person into what they were meant to be.

options.text: It highlights that God can use anyone, even a lowly slave, for good.
options.note: A fair application, but the precise point is the transformation from useless to useful in Christ.

options.text: It shows that Onesimus was always a good and useful servant.
options.note: The text says he was formerly useless; the change came through Christ.

options.text: It proves Onesimus never really ran away.
options.note: He had run away; the point is his transformation, not his innocence.

quiz.q: How does Paul ask Philemon to receive Onesimus back?

options.text: No longer as a slave but as a beloved brother, both in person and in the Lord.
options.note: Verse 16 reframes the whole relationship; brotherhood in Christ changes everything.

options.text: As a forgiven servant restored to his former place.
options.note: Forgiveness is included, but Paul asks for far more than restored servitude: welcome as a beloved brother.

options.text: As a hired worker owed wages.
options.note: Paul reframes him not as an employee but as a beloved brother.

options.text: As a slave who must be punished for running away.
options.note: Paul asks for welcome as a brother, not punishment.

quiz.q: How does this letter bear on the question of slavery?

options.text: It undermines slavery from within by making master and slave beloved brothers in Christ.
options.note: Paul plants a truth, brotherhood in the Lord, that slavery cannot finally survive, Colossians 3:11 and Galatians 3:28.

options.text: It commands the immediate legal abolition of all slavery.
options.note: Paul does not issue a decree; he plants the deeper truth that dissolves slavery from the inside.

options.text: It approves of slavery as a good institution.
options.note: Paul never calls slavery good; he transforms the relationship at its root.

options.text: It has nothing to say about how people treat one another.
options.note: The letter is precisely about how the gospel reshapes a real human relationship.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by calling Onesimus his very heart?

options.text: Onesimus is his beloved child in the faith, so dear that sending him back is like sending his own heart.
options.note: Paul uses splagchna, the deep affections, staking his own love on Onesimus, verse 12.

options.text: Onesimus had become a deeply valued helper and companion to Paul.
options.note: He was a valued helper, but the phrase conveys far deeper affection: his very heart.

options.text: Onesimus was a close relative of Paul by blood.
options.note: The bond is spiritual, not biological; Paul became his father in the faith.

options.text: It is only a figure of speech with no real feeling.
options.note: Paul's love for Onesimus is deeply felt and central to the appeal.

questions: Paul had the right to command but chose to appeal in love. Where do you hold a right over someone that love might call you to lay down?
Onesimus was useless apart from Christ and useful in Him. How has Christ begun to make you into the person you were created to be, and where do you long for more of that change?
Paul asked Philemon to see a runaway slave as a beloved brother. Is there someone you find it hard to see as family in Christ, and what would it take to receive them that way?
The gospel turned a broken relationship into brotherhood. Where do you need Christ to reframe a relationship that the world would leave in its old, hard categories?
Paul staked his own heart on Onesimus and sent him back. Who might God be calling you to advocate for, at real cost to yourself?

translationNotes.note: Onesimus means useful, per the BSB footnote, or beneficial as in verse 20; the name is the key to the wordplay in verse 11.
Useless and useful render achrestos and euchrestos, a deliberate pun on the name Onesimus; the shared root also echoes the Greek word for Christ, hinting that apart from Christ he was useless.
The BSB prints an em dash at the end of this verse before the phrase no longer as a slave in verse 16; it has been rendered as a colon here per house style, since verse 16 completes the thought.
Both in person and in the Lord, literally in the flesh and in the Lord, means Onesimus is a brother both in everyday life and in their shared union with Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Philemon 1:8-16. The em dash at the end of verse 15 has been rendered as a colon per house style.
